serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tagliatelle alla ragu is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of fresh egg tagliatelle topped with slow-cooked beef ragù and finished with grated Parmesan cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with meat sauce, homemade style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA FDC entry 'Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt' (FDC ID 20420) and 'Beef, ground, 80% lean / 20% fat, cooked' (FDC ID 174036) for macro derivation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FDC entry exists for tagliatelle alla ragu as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted combination of: cooked egg tagliatelle (~55% by weight), beef ragù sauce (~35% by weight, based on 80/20 ground beef with tomato and aromatics), and Parmesan cheese (~10% by weight). Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FDC 'Pasta with meat sauce' (FDC ID 168920) as a plausibility anchor. All values expressed per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021; pasta (egg-based, cooked) GI reference value ~45 (glucose=100 scale)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for tagliatelle alla ragu as a composite dish. GI of 45 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021, AJCN) for egg-based pasta (cooked), which is the dominant glycemic component. The meat sauce and Parmesan contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 29.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 18.5 × 3.5 = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1085 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of tagliatelle alla ragu may contribute approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~29 per restaurant serving) from egg pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low glycemic index of 45, the substantial carbohydrate content of a full restaurant serving (~65 g carbohydrate) produces a moderate glycemic load of approximately 29; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ground beef and Parmesan cheese (~2.3 g per 100 g; ~8 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat contribution from beef ragù and Parmesan (~8 g per restaurant serving) is clinically relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing leaner beef mince, reducing cheese quantity, and limiting portion size can meaningfully lower saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tagliatelle alla Ragu is a classic northern Italian pasta dish originating from the Emilia-Romagna region, most closely associated with Bologna. It consists of long, flat ribbon-shaped egg pasta (tagliatelle) served with a slow-cooked meat sauce (ragù) traditionally made from ground or minced beef, soffritto (onion, carrot, celery), tomato paste or crushed tomatoes, red wine, and milk or cream, finished with grated Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese. The pasta is made fresh from durum wheat flour and eggs, giving it a tender yet slightly chewy texture that clings well to the rich, unctuous sauce. The ragù is simmered for several hours to develop deep, savoury flavour. Nutritionally, a restaurant serving (~350 g) provides approximately 578 kcal, 32 g protein, 21 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (~45) due to the egg pasta matrix, but a moderate glycemic load per serving (~29) owing to portion size. Sodium content is notable (~1085 mg per serving) and clinically relevant for hypertension management. The saturated fat content (~8 g per serving) from beef and cheese warrants attention for cardiovascular disease risk. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy allergies. It is a culturally significant Italian comfort food widely served in Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore at establishments such as Ricciotti.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for tagliatelle alla ragu as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted combination of USDA FDC component entries (cooked egg pasta, cooked ground beef, Parmesan cheese, tomato-based sauce) and cross-checked against USDA FDC 'Pasta with meat sauce' (FDC ID 168920). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (egg-based pasta, cooked) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source database for this composite dish; trace amounts may be present from egg yolk in the pasta but are below the reporting threshold and cannot be defensibly quantified from available component data. All other sentinel -1 fields from the template have been replaced with estimated values where defensible component data was available. Nutrient values represent the finished composite dish per 100 g edible portion. Restaurant serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical Italian restaurant pasta portion norms; the actual Ricciotti serving may differ. The input record listed a price of SGD 30.52 at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, Singapore, which is consistent with a premium Italian restaurant pasta dish but price data is not used in nutritional derivation. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate for egg-based pasta per University of Sydney GI Database; the meat sauce components contribute negligible glycemic effect. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 29.1.
